serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with beancurd slices, seaweed, and sweet vinegar dressing, as typically served at a Singaporean café or fusion restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component foods: Cooked white rice (HPB Singapore Foodo), Firm tofu / beancurd (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #16126), Seaweed / wakame (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11667), Sweet vinegar dressing (estimated from rice vinegar and sugar components)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo); USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 60% cooked white rice, 20% firm tofu slices, 10% seaweed (wakame), and 10% sweet vinegar dressing. HPB Foodo used for cooked rice baseline; USDA SR Legacy used for tofu and seaweed components. Sweet vinegar dressing estimated from rice vinegar and sugar. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and composite rice dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked white rice GI (Jasmine/long-grain) and tofu/seaweed modifying effect
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet vinegar seaweed beancurd rice. Cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain) has a GI of approximately 64–72 per the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of tofu (protein and fat content) and seaweed (fibre) moderately lowers the effective GI of the composite dish. An estimated GI of 64 is applied, representing the lower bound of white rice GI, adjusted for the protein-fat-fibre dampening effect of tofu and seaweed components. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 52.3, where carbohydrate per serving = 21.5 g/100g × 380 g serving = 81.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seaweed and vinegar dressing
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 798 mg sodium (210 mg/100g × 380 g), which represents about 35% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium dressing and monitor portion size carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base and added sugar in sweet vinegar dressing
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic load of approximately 52 per serving and a medium GI of 64, this dish can cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, substitute brown rice if available, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile with beneficial soy protein and seaweed components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100g) and contains no cholesterol, with soy-derived protein from tofu and omega-3-containing seaweed providing cardioprotective benefits; however, sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Vinegar Seaweed Beancurd Slices Rice is a Singaporean fusion rice bowl dish served at contemporary cafés and fusion restaurants such as Flavours by Saute. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice topped with sliced firm beancurd (tofu), rehydrated or fresh seaweed, and a sweet vinegar dressing that provides a tangy-sweet flavour profile. The beancurd contributes a soft, silken-to-firm texture and mild soy flavour, while the seaweed adds a briny, umami-rich note and slight chewiness. The sweet vinegar dressing — typically made from rice vinegar, sugar, and seasoning — ties the components together with a light, refreshing acidity. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (approximately 118 kcal per 100g), relatively low in fat, and provides plant-based protein from tofu and iodine-rich minerals from seaweed. It is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and is dairy-free. Clinically, the dish carries a moderate glycemic load due to the white rice base and added sugar in the dressing, making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content from seaweed and dressing warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is broadly heart-friendly due to its low saturated fat content and absence of cholesterol, with tofu providing isoflavones associated with cardiovascular benefit.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Singapore Foodo (cooked white rice) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (firm tofu, wakame seaweed). The GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the protein-fat-fibre dampening effect of tofu and seaweed. Medium confidence reflects the estimation approach rather than a direct measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated (not directly measured): approximately 60% cooked white rice, 20% firm tofu, 10% seaweed (wakame), 10% sweet vinegar dressing. Direct laboratory analysis would improve confidence to high. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by component analysis. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all components (rice, tofu, seaweed) are plant-derived and contain no cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; some seaweed varieties contain trace pseudovitamin B12 analogues that are not bioavailable. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in this plant-based dish. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), potassium_mg (120.0), calcium_mg (85.0), iron_mg (1.1), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (4.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.05), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.04), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.07), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.3), phosphorus_mg (95.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (0.5), selenium_mcg (8.0), water_g (70.5) are all estimated from component USDA SR Legacy values and are not directly measured. 7. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish has not been performed. 8. The cuisine_tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and do not reflect the nature of this dish; they have been disregarded in this record. 9. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate for a standard Singapore café rice bowl portion.
